clean_story:
Brain’s guesswork engine. Reality as feedback.

The term predictive processing describes how your brain constantly predicts what will happen next—like a weather forecaster for your senses. It doesn’t just react; it expects.

Your brain builds a model of the world, then checks it against incoming signals. If the prediction matches (e.g., "that shadow is a tree"), it ignores the details. If not (e.g., "the shadow moves—it’s a cat!"), it updates fast. This saves energy: processing every leaf’s rustle would overload you. Instead, your brain fills gaps with educated guesses.

Example: Read this:
"The brin filsl in the blnaks with exepcted letetrs."
Your brain corrects the typos because it predicts "brain fills in the blanks." Mispredict? You stumble—like when a stair has one fewer step than expected.

Even emotions work this way. A racing heart could mean "excitement" or "danger"—your brain picks the prediction that fits past experience.

Three related subjects are how illusions trick perception, AI’s prediction algorithms, and why déjà vu feels familiar.
